class_schedule.topic: Why Theory? The Unavoidability of Interpretation
learning_objectives: Understand why literary theory is not an optional add-on but an unavoidable dimension of all reading
Identify the basic questions that distinguish major theoretical schools
Analyze how different theoretical lenses produce different readings of the same text
readings: Eagleton, Literary Theory: An Introduction, Preface and Ch. 1

lecture_notes.introduction: Every time you read a text, you are already using theory — assumptions about what counts as meaning, where meaning comes from, and what the purpose of reading is. This course makes those assumptions visible so you can use them deliberately.
key_concepts: Theory is unavoidable: every reading rests on assumptions about meaning, value, and method
The major questions: What does the text mean? Who decides? What counts as evidence?
Schools of criticism as different answers to the same questions
Theory vs. method: a theoretical framework generates specific analytical practices
The anxiety about theory: why some readers resist it and why that resistance is itself theoretical
lecture_notes.demonstration: Reading Frost's 'The Road Not Taken' through four different lenses (formalist, biographical, Marxist, deconstructionist) to show how the same text yields radically different interpretations

review.value: There is no neutral, theory-free reading; even the claim to 'just read the text' is a theoretical position (formalism) with specific assumptions about meaning and value
Each theoretical school asks different questions, foregrounds different textual features, and produces different kinds of knowledge — no single school has a monopoly on truth

class_schedule.topic: Formalism and New Criticism: The Text Itself
learning_objectives: Explain the New Critical method: close reading, the intentional fallacy, the affective fallacy
Analyze how formalism isolates the text from author and reader to focus on internal structures
Identify the strengths and limitations of formalist approaches
readings: Keats, 'Ode on a Grecian Urn' (for applied analysis)

lecture_notes.introduction: New Criticism dominated American literary studies for decades. Its central claim is radical: the meaning of a poem is in the poem itself — not in the author's intention, not in the reader's response, not in the historical context.
key_concepts: The intentional fallacy: the author's stated intention is irrelevant to the text's meaning
The affective fallacy: the reader's emotional response is not evidence of the text's meaning
Close reading as method: attending to irony, paradox, ambiguity, and tension within the text
The well-wrought urn: the poem as a self-contained, internally coherent verbal structure
Limitations: what formalism cannot see — history, politics, ideology, identity
lecture_notes.demonstration: New Critical reading of Keats's 'Ode on a Grecian Urn,' analyzing how paradox and irony structure the poem's argument, then discussing what this reading gains and what it misses

review.value: Wimsatt and Beardsley's argument that the author's intended meaning should not determine interpretation, because the text is a public artifact whose meaning resides in its language, not its creator's mind

Brooks's argument that a poem's meaning cannot be separated from its form — to paraphrase a poem is to destroy it, because the meaning IS the particular arrangement of words, not a content extractable from them

class_schedule.topic: Structuralism and Post-Structuralism: Language and Difference
learning_objectives: Explain Saussure's theory of the sign and its implications for literary meaning
Analyze Derrida's concept of differance and its challenge to stable meaning
Understand deconstruction as a reading practice, not a nihilistic destruction of meaning
readings: Saussure, Course in General Linguistics (selections on the sign)
Derrida, 'Structure, Sign, and Play in the Discourse of the Human Sciences' (excerpt)

lecture_notes.introduction: Structuralism argued that meaning comes from systems of difference, not from reference to reality. Post-structuralism radicalized this insight: if meaning depends on difference, it can never be fixed, because the chain of differences never ends.
key_concepts: Saussure's sign: signifier (sound-image) and signified (concept) are arbitrarily connected
Language as a system of differences: a word means what it means by not being other words
Binary oppositions: structuralism reveals the paired oppositions that organize meaning (nature/culture, male/female, speech/writing)
Differance: Derrida's term for the endless deferral and differing that prevents meaning from ever being fully present
Deconstruction in practice: finding the moments where a text undermines its own apparent argument
lecture_notes.demonstration: Deconstructive reading of a short passage from Rousseau, showing how the text's defense of 'natural' speech over 'artificial' writing depends on the very writing it condemns

review.value: Saussure's foundational insight: there is no natural connection between a word and what it represents; 'tree' means tree only because English speakers agree it does, not because anything about the sound resembles a tree

Derrida's neologism combining 'to differ' and 'to defer': meaning is produced by differences between signs, and because this process is endless, meaning is always deferred, never fully present — a fundamental challenge to the idea of fixed textual meaning

class_schedule.topic: Marxist Criticism: Literature, Ideology, and Class
learning_objectives: Analyze how Marxist criticism reads literature as a product and agent of ideological struggle
Understand the concepts of ideology, hegemony, and the base/superstructure model
Apply Marxist reading practices to a literary text
readings: Eagleton, Literary Theory, Ch. 1 ('The Rise of English')

lecture_notes.introduction: Marxist criticism asks: whose interests does this text serve? Literature is not a transparent window on reality — it is produced within economic and social systems that shape what can be written, published, read, and valued.
key_concepts: Base and superstructure: the economic base shapes the cultural superstructure, including literature
Ideology: the system of beliefs that makes the existing social order appear natural and inevitable
Hegemony: Gramsci's concept of domination through consent rather than force
The literary canon as ideological formation: who decides what counts as great literature and why
Reading against the grain: finding the text's ideological contradictions and silences
lecture_notes.demonstration: Marxist reading of 'Ozymandias,' analyzing how the poem's apparent message about the fall of tyrants coexists with its own participation in literary canon-building and the cultural authority of the Romantic poet

review.value: Althusser's concept: ideology is not false consciousness but the system of representations through which individuals experience their relationship to the real conditions of existence — it constitutes subjects rather than deceiving them

A Marxist practice of reading texts not for their surface meaning but for their ideological contradictions — the moments where the text's stated values conflict with its structural assumptions about class, labor, and power

class_schedule.topic: Feminist and Gender Criticism: Reading as a Woman
learning_objectives: Analyze how feminist criticism reveals the gendered assumptions embedded in literary texts and literary institutions
Understand the evolution of feminist criticism from images-of-women criticism to intersectional analysis
Apply gender-based reading practices to both canonical and non-canonical texts
readings: Woolf, A Room of One's Own, Ch. 1-3
Gilbert and Gubar, The Madwoman in the Attic, Introduction

lecture_notes.introduction: Feminist criticism begins with a deceptively simple question: what happens when we read as women? The answers have transformed literary studies by revealing how deeply gender shapes what gets written, published, read, and valued.
key_concepts: Woolf's material analysis: women need money and a room of their own to write
The angel in the house: the Victorian ideal of femininity as an obstacle to women's creative freedom
Images-of-women criticism: analyzing how women are represented in male-authored texts
Gynocriticism: the study of women's writing as a distinct literary tradition
Intersectionality: how gender intersects with race, class, sexuality, and other axes of identity
lecture_notes.demonstration: Feminist reading of the Wife of Bath's Prologue (Chaucer), analyzing whether the Wife is a proto-feminist voice or a misogynist caricature, and how the answer depends on whether we read through the character or through the male author

review.value: Woolf's argument that women's exclusion from literature is material, not intellectual: denied education, property, and privacy, women could not write — and when they did, their work was dismissed or forgotten

The recognition that gender does not operate in isolation: a Black woman's experience of patriarchy differs from a white woman's, and feminist criticism must account for the intersections of gender with race, class, sexuality, and other identities

questions.question: Explain the 'intentional fallacy' as defined by Wimsatt and Beardsley. Why did New Critics consider the author's intention irrelevant to interpretation?
questions.answer: The intentional fallacy is the error of judging a literary work by the author's stated purpose. New Critics argued that once a text is published, it becomes a public artifact whose meaning resides in its language, not in the author's mind. The author's intention is either already realized in the text (and therefore redundant) or not realized (and therefore irrelevant). Meaning must be determined from the text itself.

rubric.key: Accurate definition of the intentional fallacy

questions.question: What is Saussure's concept of the 'arbitrary sign,' and what are its implications for how meaning works in language?
questions.answer: Saussure argued that the relationship between the signifier (sound-image) and the signified (concept) is arbitrary — there is no natural or necessary connection between the word 'tree' and the concept of a tree. This means meaning is not based on reference to reality but on differences within a system: 'tree' means what it means because it is not 'free' or 'three.' Language is a system of differences without positive terms.

rubric.key: Explains meaning as based on difference, not reference
Provides an example illustrating the concept

questions.question: What does Althusser mean by 'ideology,' and how does his concept differ from the common usage of the word as 'false beliefs'?
questions.answer: Althusser defines ideology not as false consciousness (mistaken beliefs about reality) but as the system of representations through which individuals experience their relationship to the real conditions of existence. Ideology is not something imposed from above that can be corrected by truth; it constitutes subjects — it shapes how people understand themselves and their world. It operates through institutions (schools, churches, media) rather than through individual deception.

rubric.key: Distinguishes Althusser's concept from false consciousness
Explains ideology as constitutive of subjects

questions.question: According to Virginia Woolf in A Room of One's Own, why have women historically been underrepresented in the literary canon? Is her argument primarily about talent or about material conditions?
questions.answer: Woolf's argument is primarily about material conditions, not talent. She argues that women were denied the education, financial independence, and private space necessary to write. Her thought experiment about 'Shakespeare's sister' — a woman with equal genius who would have been denied every opportunity available to her brother — demonstrates that the absence of women from literary history reflects social exclusion, not intellectual inferiority.

rubric.key: Identifies the argument as material, not about talent
References the Shakespeare's sister thought experiment
Connects education, money, and space to literary production

major_assignments.title: Applied Theory Essay
major_assignments.description: Write a 2,500-3,000 word essay that applies a specific theoretical framework to a literary text of your choice. The essay must demonstrate genuine understanding of the theory, not merely name-drop theoretical concepts, and must show how the theory reveals something about the text that other approaches would miss.
objectives: Apply a theoretical framework to a literary text with sophistication
Demonstrate genuine understanding of the theory's premises and methods
Show what the theory reveals that other approaches would miss
Acknowledge the theory's limitations as well as its insights
deliverables: Works Cited with primary text, theoretical sources, and at least 2 secondary sources
500-word reflection on what the chosen theory reveals and what it misses

criteria: Theory's core concepts are accurately represented
Application demonstrates genuine understanding, not mechanical application
Student distinguishes their own analysis from the theorist's framework
Limitations of the approach are acknowledged
Close reading is detailed and accurate
Theory illuminates the text rather than being imposed on it
Analysis reveals something a non-theoretical reading would miss
Textual evidence is well-chosen and carefully analyzed
Complex theoretical concepts are explained clearly
Sophisticated application of theory revealing new textual insights
Genuine understanding of theoretical premises and methods
Detailed close reading grounded in the chosen framework
Clear prose making complex ideas accessible

applied_theory_essay.description: Essay applying a theoretical framework to a literary text

theory_journal.description: Weekly journal entries summarizing and responding to theoretical readings

quizzes.description: Quizzes on theoretical concepts and key terms

seminar_participation.description: Quality of seminar discussion and theoretical argumentation

final_examination.description: Take-home exam applying multiple theories to unseen texts

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory. This is a seminar course; more than 2 unexcused absences result in a letter grade deduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Essays penalized 5% per day late. Theory journals not accepted late.
course_policies.collaboration: All written work is individual. Seminar discussion is collaborative and must be conducted with intellectual rigor and respect for disagreement.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Plagiarism results in a zero and referral to the academic integrity board.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Course reader with theoretical texts, word processing software, library database access, and LMS access.
